module.name: scanner
module.spec: FileScanner follows imports starting from main.drift.xml, parses each .drift.xml as XML, extracts spec elements with their id attribute, processes inner content to extract spec-spec edges (recording each <ref spec="…"> as an Edge with From = the containing spec's module-qualified ID and To = the referenced ID), SHA1-hashes the canonical content (refs stripped, label text preserved), and returns Spec records with module-qualified ID, Hash, Filepath, and LineNumber alongside the discovered spec-spec Edges. See principles.language_agnostic.

FileScanner walks the project directory for text files (see text_file_detection), finds lines matching the D! id=shortcode range-start and D! id=shortcode range-end pattern, pairs them by ID within the same file, SHA1-hashes the lines between range-start and range-end (exclusive of both marker lines) with other marker declarations blanked, and returns Marker records with ID, Hash, Filepath, LineNumber, and EndLineNumber. See principles.language_agnostic.
All hashing uses SHA1 hex-encoded via crypto/sha1 and fmt percent-x formatting. The hash is deterministic across runs for identical content.
Duplicate spec IDs within the same module cause an error with the duplicate ID quoted. See principles.specs_are_truth.
Duplicate marker shortcodes across files cause an error with the duplicate shortcode quoted. See principles.specs_are_truth.
A spec element without an id attribute causes an error. See principles.specs_are_truth.

The scanner scans ALL text files for markers — there is no language allowlist. Any file that is not a recognized binary format (checked by extension blocklist) and does not contain null bytes in its first 8KB (checked by content sampling) is treated as a text file and scanned for the D! marker pattern. This makes drift language-agnostic: any file with a comment line containing D! id=... is a valid marker host, regardless of extension. Binary files (images, archives, executables, etc.) are skipped by extension blocklist first (fast path, avoids reading the file) and by null-byte detection as a fallback (catches unknown binary formats). The .drift/ directory and drift ignore patterns also exclude files from scanning. See principles.language_agnostic for the design commitment.

If spec elements are nested inside a specs wrapper element under main or module, the scanner returns an error explaining that specs must be direct children of the root element, not inside a specs wrapper. See principles.specs_are_truth.

The id attribute in a spec element must not contain a dot. The scanner constructs the fully-qualified spec ID as moduleName + "." + localId. This ensures every qualified spec ID has exactly one dot, separating module from local ID.

Marker shortcodes must match the pattern [A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_]* (alphanumeric, starting with a letter, no dots or punctuation). This is enforced by the marker regex itself, not post-match validation — lines containing D! id= with an invalid shortcode (e.g. containing a dot) simply do not match and are silently ignored. This avoids false positives on documentation that mentions the marker syntax. Dots are reserved for spec ID qualification, enabling unambiguous disambiguation in CLI commands. See principles.language_agnostic.

Every marker must declare a range-start or range-end suffix as part of the marker line. This is enforced by the marker regex (the suffix is required for a match, not optional) — lines matching D! id=foo without a suffix do not match and are silently ignored. Each range-start with ID X must have a matching range-end with ID X in the same file appearing after the start. Each range-end with ID X must have a matching range-start with ID X in the same file appearing before the end. The scanner reports all unpaired markers at once, not fail-on-first.

Before hashing a marker's range, every line within the range that matches the marker pattern has its D! declaration stripped (from D! to end of line), leaving only the comment prefix. This makes markers invisible to each other's hashes, enabling nested and overlapping ranges without interference.

processSpecContent extracts <ref spec="…"> edges from a spec's innerxml using a strict regex grammar: the spec attribute must use double quotes and appear as the first attribute, in either self-closing form (<ref spec="X" />) or paired form (<ref spec="X">label</ref>). It returns the canonical content (each ref replaced by its label text, or removed entirely for self-closing refs) plus the list of referenced spec IDs in source order. Duplicate (from, to) pairs within the same spec are deduped at the scanner level so validation never sees them. Malformed <ref> tags (typos like <red spec="X"> or missing spec attribute) cause a scan error; refs to nonexistent spec IDs do NOT error here — they surface as TodoBrokenEdge drift items. See principles.specs_are_truth.

Overview: ReadSpecContent reads a .drift.xml file and returns the trimmed inner text of the <spec> element whose id attribute matches the local part (post-dot suffix) of a fully-qualified spec ID. Used by drift diff, drift show, drift list --verbose, and writeBaseline to read or seed baseline content snapshots.
Defined terms: specID — fully-qualified spec ID (e.g. "core.validate"); the local part is the substring after the first dot localID — the local part of specID (e.g. "validate")
Requirements: R1. ReadSpecContent(filepath, specID) MUST return an error if specID does not contain a dot. R2. ReadSpecContent MUST parse the file as XML and locate the first <spec> element whose id attribute equals localID. R3. ReadSpecContent MUST return the trimmed inner content of the matched spec element. Trimming removes leading and trailing whitespace. R4. If no matching spec element exists in the file, ReadSpecContent MUST return an error naming the specID and filepath. R5. ReadSpecContent MUST NOT strip <ref> tags from the returned content. Refs are stripped only for canonical hashing (see scanner.spec_discovery); display content preserves them verbatim.
See also: scanner.spec_discovery, build.show_result, pinstore.baselines.

Overview: ReadMarkerContent reads a code file and returns the lines between a marker's range-start and range-end, with nested marker declarations blanked. Used by drift diff, drift show, drift list --verbose, and writeBaseline.
Defined terms: startLine — the line number (1-indexed) immediately after the range-start marker endLine — the line number (1-indexed) of the range-end marker line itself half-open range — [startLine, endLine-1]; excludes both marker lines
Requirements: R1. ReadMarkerContent(filepath, startLine, endLine) MUST return the lines in the half-open range [startLine, endLine-1], 1-indexed. R2. Lines within the range that contain a nested "D! id=" marker declaration MUST have that declaration stripped (from "D!" to end of line) but the remainder of the line preserved. R3. If the range extends past the end of the file, ReadMarkerContent MUST return only the available lines without error. R4. The returned content MUST end with a trailing newline when at least one line is returned; an empty result MUST be the empty string (no trailing newline). R5. ReadMarkerContent MUST NOT strip lines matching the parent range-start or range-end markers — those are excluded by the half-open range definition.
See also: scanner.marker_discovery, scanner.range_markers, scanner.marker_blanking, build.show_result.
